coredata.dc:title: β-Cyclodextrin/carbon dots-grafted cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel for enhanced adsorption and fluorescence detection of levofloxacin

coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel hydrogel, β-cyclodextrin/carbon dots-grafted cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel (βCCH), was fabricated for removal and fluorescence determination of levofloxacin (LEV). A comprehensive analysis was performed to characterize its physicochemical properties. Batch adsorption experiments were conducted, revealing that βCCH reached a maximum adsorption capacity of 1376.9 mg/g, consistent with both Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models, suggesting that the adsorption process of LEV on βCCH was primarily driven by chemical adsorption. The removal efficiency of βCCH was 99.2 % under the fixed conditions (pH: 6, initial concentration: 20 mg/L, contact time: 300 min, temperature: 25 °C). The removal efficiency of βCCH for LEV still achieved 97.3 % after five adsorption-desorption cycles. By using βCCH as a fluorescent probe for LEV, a fast and sensitive method was established with linear ranges of 1–120 mg/L and 0.2–1.0 μg/L and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.09 μg/L. The viability of βCCH was estimated based on the economic analysis of the synthesis process and the removal of LEV, demonstrating that βCCH was more cost-effective than commercial activated carbon. This study provides a novel approach for preparing a promising antibiotic detection and adsorption material with the advantages of stability, and cost-effectiveness.

head.ce:title: β-Cyclodextrin/carbon dots-grafted cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel for enhanced adsorption and fluorescence detection of levofloxacin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel hydrogel, β-cyclodextrin/carbon dots-grafted cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel (βCCH), was fabricated for removal and fluorescence determination of levofloxacin (LEV). A comprehensive analysis was performed to characterize its physicochemical properties. Batch adsorption experiments were conducted, revealing that βCCH reached a maximum adsorption capacity of 1376.9 mg/g, consistent with both Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models, suggesting that the adsorption process of LEV on βCCH was primarily driven by chemical adsorption. The removal efficiency of βCCH was 99.2 % under the fixed conditions (pH: 6, initial concentration: 20 mg/L, contact time: 300 min, temperature: 25 °C). The removal efficiency of βCCH for LEV still achieved 97.3 % after five adsorption-desorption cycles. By using βCCH as a fluorescent probe for LEV, a fast and sensitive method was established with linear ranges of 1–120 mg/L and 0.2–1.0 μg/L and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.09 μg/L. The viability of βCCH was estimated based on the economic analysis of the synthesis process and the removal of LEV, demonstrating that βCCH was more cost-effective than commercial activated carbon. This study provides a novel approach for preparing a promising antibiotic detection and adsorption material with the advantages of stability, and cost-effectiveness.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Levofloxacin (LEV) is one of the third-generation quinolone antibiotics, which is widely employed to treat genitourinary diseases, gastrointestinal diseases, and infections caused by Gram-negative bacteria in human and animal skin tissues (Sun et al., 2019). The residue of LEV not only contributes to bacterial resistance and affects aquatic life but also poses potential threats to human health through the food chain, seriously disrupting human physiological functions (Song et al., 2022). Therefore, the effective removal of LEV from water is of significant importance (Yang et al., 2022).

Notably, adsorption is high efficiency, cost-effective, and easy to control, making it a very suitable technology for antibiotic removal (Mangla et al., 2022). The efficiency of adsorption depends significantly on the types of materials and their characteristics. In the adsorptive removal of antibiotics, the potential of various adsorbents has been explored, including hydrogels. Hydrogels, known for their hydrophilic nature and three-dimensional (3D) porous structure, have garnered significant attention as adsorbents in wastewater treatment (Sharma et al., 2022). Juengchareonpoon et al. (2019) demonstrated that a hydrogel composed of β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethylcellulose had the capability to remove oxytetracycline. The experimental results indicated that the adsorption capacity depended on the content of β-cyclodextrin. Among the raw materials for hydrogel synthesis, β-cyclodextrin, a cyclic oligosaccharide comprising seven glucose units, has several advantages in the preparation of hydrogels. It is capable of forming inclusion complexes with various guest molecules and possesses versatility, biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly characteristics (Roy et al., 2023). For example, Song et al. (2024) synthesized a carboxyl-functionalized β-cyclodextrin-based hydrogel with an adsorption capacity of 108.81 mg/g for antibiotics such as tetracycline hydrochloride. Tian et al. (2023) prepared poly-β-cyclodextrin/polyvinyl alcohol/polyacrylic acid hydrogel, and determined that the resulting hydrogel exhibited a maximum equilibrium adsorption capacity of 372.12 mg/g for ciprofloxacin. The provided examples underscore the potential benefits of β-cyclodextrin-based hydrogels in antibiotic adsorption. Additionally, the β-cyclodextrin-based hydrogel can demonstrate considerable promise for efficient adsorption of LEV. Cellulose nanofibrils (CNs) are nano-sized cellulose, abundantly present in nature and obtained from renewable sources such as wood pulp, agricultural residues, or other biomass. CNs boast a high aspect ratio and a substantial surface area, offering abundant active sites for adsorption (Curvello et al., 2019). Consequently, CNs possess the capability to augment the adsorption capacity of β-cyclodextrin-based hydrogel, rendering them efficacious in the capture and removal of LEV.

Currently, various analytical techniques, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), HPLC-mass spectrometry, bio-electrochemical methods, and thin layer chromatography, have been employed for LEV detection (Dafale et al., 2015; Ghanbari et al., 2019). Despite the high sensitivity and accuracy exhibited by these methods, they are often hindered by complex pre-treatment procedures, extended assay durations, operational challenges, and elevated costs. In response to these limitations, fluorescence sensing has garnered increasing attention. This method offers notable advantages, such as rapid response times, convenient detection procedures, simplicity in operation, and heightened sensitivity (Xu et al., 2022). For example, Korah et al. (2022) synthesized novel CDs using a facile hydrothermal method derived from Curcuma amada. Subsequently, they demonstrated the CDs' capability to selectively detect LEV with a lower detection limit of 2 nM and a linear range spanning from 0 to 16 μM. Li et al. (2018) synthesized uniform nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped CDs through a one-step microwave-assisted method, which was subsequently employed for the determination of LEV. Furthermore, Mathew et al. (2023) utilized Coleus zeylanicus leaves to synthesize novel nitrogen-doped CDs. They demonstrated that these CDs functioned both as a fluorescent probe for LEV detection and as an electrochemical sensing probe for the detection of amoxicillin. These examples underscore the significant benefits of CDs in LEV detection. However, the practical application of CDs encounters challenges, primarily due to their small interparticle distances, leading to easy aggregation. To mitigate these issues, loading CDs onto a carrier, particularly a β-cyclodextrin-based hydrogel, emerges as a promising solution.

In summary, β-cyclodextrin, CNs, and CDs possess favorable properties. However, their combined effect on LEV removal is not yet fully understood (Abukhadra et al., 2022; Mashile et al., 2020), and there is a lack of a synthetic approach to transform them into an adsorbent. Hence, we hypothesized that harnessing β-cyclodextrin, CNs, and CDs as base nanomaterials could yield a groundbreaking βCCH for enhanced adsorption and fluorescence detection of LEV. Characterization techniques, including scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), UV–vis absorption, and photoluminescence (PL), verified successful synthesis of βCCH. Factors influencing LEV adsorption on βCCH, such as pH, temperature (T), initial concentration (C0), and contact time (t), were thoroughly discussed. Additionally, βCCH exhibited significant fluorescence quenching by LEV, leading to its application in LEV detection. The adsorption and sensing mechanism of βCCH for LEV was also elucidated. Furthermore, a cost analysis of βCCH for LEV adsorption was conducted.
The materials and reagents are detailed in Supplementary materials.

For the preparation of CNs, bleached wood pulp (BWP) was the starting material. The main component of the BWP was cellulose; other components, including lignin, and hemicelluloses, were largely removed. Here, 5.0 g of BWP was suspended in 300 mL of deionized (DI) water through mechanical stirring for 30 min. In a separate process, TEMPO (0.5 mmol) and NaBr (5 mmol) were dissolved in 200 mL of DI water with magnetic stirring for 30 min. This solution was slowly added to the pulp suspension. Subsequently, a specific quantity of NaClO (30 mmol) was gradually added. The mixture was kept at room temperature for 7 h while maintaining the pH within the range of 10. Following this, 5 mL of ethanol was added to terminate the oxidation reaction. The oxidized BWP underwent washing with DI water until the pH reached 7. Eventually, the oxidized BWP was homogenized five times using a high-pressure homogenizer to obtain CNs, which were stored at 4 °C for subsequent use. Table S1 presents the comparison of various types of nanocellulose.

A total of 2.0 g of ammonium citrate (AC) and 1.0 g of L-Cysteine were dissolved in 10 mL of DI water through 30 min of ultrasonic treatment, followed by heating in a domestic microwave oven at a power of 700 W for 5 min. After the reaction, the resulting mixture was evaporated to obtain a powder. The powder was ground in a mortar, dissolved in ethanol, and the solution was then centrifuged. The supernatant was collected, subjected to filtration using a 0.22 μm membrane filter, and the filtrate was further diluted to 100 mL. The final CDs was stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

Various amounts of CDs (25 μL, 50 μL, 100 μL, 200 μL, 400 μL, 800 μL, and 1600 μL), maintaining a consistent concentration of 0.5 wt%, were combined with 10.0 g of CNs suspension (1.0 wt%) and homogenized using 15 min of ultrasonic treatment for uniformity. The mixture was stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 25 °C for 24 h. Consequently, CDs@CNs-x (where x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7) were prepared.

An L16 orthogonal array experimental design was utilized, analyzing four variables: β-cyclodextrin (factor A), AA (factor B), CDs@CNs (factor C), and MBA (factor D). Each factor included three levels (refer to Table S2 for details). Following the orthogonality principle, an L9 (34) test matrix was constructed to examine their impact on the adsorption capacity (Qe) of the prepared hydrogel (refer to Table S3). The optimal preparation parameters for the hydrogel were determined based on the results presented in Table S3.

Using optimal preparation parameters, the process involved dissolving 0.1 g of β-cyclodextrin in 5 mL of DI water by stirring at 75 °C. Then, 2.0 g of AA and 1.8 g of CDs@CNs-5 suspension (1.0 wt%) were added. Following this, 0.01 g of MBA and 0.01 g of PPS were introduced, followed by ultrasonication for 30 min. The resulting mixture was placed into test tubes and incubated in a water bath at 60 °C for 1 h, yielding the target product, βCCH-5. Finally, βCCH-5 underwent thorough washing with ultrapure water three times to eliminate unreacted reagents. Corresponding hydrogels were labeled as βCCH-x (x = 1 to 7) based on the utilized CDs@CNs-x (x = 1 to 7). Additionally, a control lacking CDs was denoted as βCH-5, and another, lacking both CDs and CNs, was prepared and labeled as βH. The hydrogels were formed into long cylindrical rods and subsequently cut into discs with a diameter of 12 mm and a thickness of 10 mm.

This study employed wet hydrogel doses of 30 mg in LEV solutions, with a LEV solution volume of 100 mL, and adsorption experiments were conducted at a stirring speed of 150 rpm. Unless otherwise specified, the C0 was 60 mg/L, pH was maintained at 6, and the T was set to 25 °C. Following the adsorption process, the supernatant was collected through filtration, and the LEV concentration was measured at 288 nm wavelength using an UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing East-West Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd., China). Batch experiments were employed to determine adsorption isotherms at different LEV concentrations (20–100 mg/L), while batch experiments with varied contact times (0–300 min) provided the adsorption kinetic data. The impact of pH on adsorption was studied at pH 2–10, and the effect of temperature on adsorption was assessed at 15–55 °C. All experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the results represent the mean of at least three independent measurements.

For assessing reusability, multiple cycles of adsorption and desorption were performed. βCCH-5 was easily recovered from the reaction vessel and subsequently soaked in the eluent for 5 h. After elution, βCCH-5 was washed five times with ultrapure water. The recovered βCCH-5 was then reused for another cycle of adsorption and desorption.

Cross-sectional micrographs of the prepared samples were obtained using SEM (JEOL JEM-F200, Japan). FTIR analysis was conducted using a Shimadzu instrument (Japan) with a scanning speed of 32 times/s and a resolution of 4 cm−1. The crystal structure was examined using XRD (Bruker AXS, Germany). UV–vis spectroscopy was performed on the samples using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze elemental composition of the sample, utilizing a Thermo instrument (USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Hitachi High-Technology Co., Japan.

The preparation process of βCCH-5 is depicted in Fig. 1 . βCCH-5 consists of three main components: β-cyclodextrin, CNs, and CDs. Initially, CDs were synthesized via a one-step microwave-assisted method (Li et al., 2018). Then, CNs were synthesized through a combination of TEMPO oxidation and high-pressure homogenization. Subsequently, CNs were connected with CDs to form CDs@CNs via hydrogen bonding (Huang et al., 2014). Next, CDs@CNs and β-cyclodextrin were grafted onto the AA. Furthermore, with the help of MBA, the 3D porous structure of βCCH-5 was formed.

SEM was utilized to examine the microstructure and morphologies of CNs, CDs@CNs, and βCCH-5. In Fig. 2a, CNs had relatively smooth surfaces (Yue et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 2b, the shape of CDs was homogeneous and spherical, with an average particle size of 6.4 ± 0.2nm. Additionally, the XRD spectrum of CDs showed a distinct peak at approximately 28°, indicating the predominantly amorphous structure (Fig. S1) (Sharanappa et al., 2023). Meanwhile, the smoothness of CDs@CNs was reduced, and obvious wrinkles and cracks appeared (Fig. 2c) (Qi et al., 2023). βCCH-5 showcased a distinctive 3D porous structure, as observed in Fig. 2d. SEM images of CNs, CDs@CNs, and βCCH-5 at various magnifications were obtained (Fig. S2). Additionally, elemental component analysis was performed on βCCH-5 utilizing the EDS method (Fig. S3), revealing a uniform distribution of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) (Wang et al., 2022). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm determined the specific surface area of the prepared βCCH-5 (Fig. S4). The measured specific surface area of βCCH-5 was 18.2852 m2/g. It was evident that βCCH-5 exhibited a ‘type-III’ isotherm with an ‘H3’ hysteresis loop, indicating its mesoporous nature, enhancing its adsorption capacity (Sharma, Sharma, Laddha, Agarwal, & Gupta, 2023).

The XRD patterns for β-cyclodextrin, CNs, CDs@CNs, and βCCH-5 are illustrated in Fig. 2e. β-cyclodextrin exhibits eight characteristic peaks at 2θ diffraction values of 8.9°, 10.5°, 12.3°, 15.3°, 18.9°, 20.9°, 22.6°, and 26.9°, showing cage-like structural buildup (Chakraborty et al., 2019; Das & Subuddhi, 2015). In the XRD patterns of CNs and CDs@CNs, identical peaks (2θ = 17.5° and 22.7°) align with the (110) and (200) crystal faces of cellulose (Xu et al., 2018). Remarkably, the characteristic peaks of β-cyclodextrin in the XRD pattern of βCCH-5 disappeared, indicating graft polymerization between β-cyclodextrin and AA, as graft polymerization on β-cyclodextrin can disrupt its original crystallinity (Wang, Guo, et al., 2019). However, these peaks at 17.5° and 22.7° could not be found distinctly in the XRD pattern of βCCH-5 because CDs@CNs underwent polymerization (Shahzamani et al., 2020).

The FTIR spectra of β-cyclodextrin, CNs, CDs@CNs, and βCCH-5 are shown in Fig. 2f. β-cyclodextrin had two peaks at 3383cm−1 and 1638cm−1, which were attributed to stretching vibration of the –OH (Hu & Zou, 2017). The peaks at 1155 and 1036 cm−1 were attributed to C–O–C groups and CO groups, respectively (Zhou et al., 2016). In the spectra of CNs and CDs@CNs, the peak at 1053 cm−1 was attributed to C − O − C stretching vibration (Luo et al., 2021). Compared to CNs, a new peak of 1709cm−1 appeared in the CDs@CNs spectrum, which belonged to CO stretching vibration of α, β-unsaturated carboxylic acid (Anjana et al., 2018). This change indicated that the CDs were connected to CNs via hydrogen bonds (Huang et al., 2014). The spectrum of βCCH-5 showed two bands at 1155 and 1036 cm−1 decreased, indicating that β-cyclodextrin was grafted on the AA (Jiang et al., 2023). Meanwhile, the band at 1053 cm−1 weakened, confirming the participation of CDs@CNs in the graft polymerization (Zhang et al., 2019).

The TGA curves of β-cyclodextrin, CDs@CNs, and βCCH-5 are depicted in Fig. S5. Initial weight loss, observed between 30 and 160 °C, may be attributed to the removal of physically adsorbed water. A subsequent weight loss, occurring between 200 and 480 °C, was attributed to the degradation of polysaccharide chains (Ghorpade et al., 2017). Furthermore, βCCH-5 exhibited the slowest thermal decomposition compared to the other two samples, attributed to the construction of a 3D porous structure, enhancing the thermal stability of βCCH-5 (Barros et al., 2015).

Fig. 2g illustrates the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of βCCH-5. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of βCCH-5 exhibited one peak at 255 nm, attributed to the π → π* transitions (Alhokbany et al., 2019). For the PL spectra of βCCH-5, the excitation wavelength was 345 nm, with the corresponding emission peak centered at 440 nm. The PL emission spectra, depicted in Fig. 2h, revealed varying excitation wavelengths from 315 to 375 nm, with emission peak in the range of 427 to 458 nm. Notably, the emission peak shifted towards the long wavelength with an increase in excitation wavelength (Lv et al., 2022).

Fig. 2i depicts the color changes of βCCH-x under visible and UV light at 365 nm. These variations in color may be attributed to differences in the content of CDs. Notably, among the βCCH samples, βCCH-5 exhibited the brightest fluorescence, with an absolute quantum yield (AQY0) calculated at 6.2 %. The AQY0 measurement was provided in Supporting material. The photostability of the prepared CDs and βCCH-5 were measured under 365 nm excitation using a 6 W UV lamp for different time periods, and the results are shown in Fig. S6. The results demonstrated that βCCH-5 exhibited greater stability under prolonged excitation. This phenomenon may be attributed to the 3D porous structure of βCCH-5, enhancing its photostability (Cheng et al., 2019).

The optimization of LEV adsorption was conducted utilizing response surface methodology (Yang, Zheng, et al., 2020). Three crucial factors—pH, T, and C0—were systematically considered, as detailed in Table S4. The experimental design matrix consisted of 17 runs, outlined in Table S5. Comprehensive results of the analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the F-test can be found in Table S6. The regression coefficient (R2) was 0.9956, indicating a strong correlation between predicted and experimental results (Fig. S7). Significance of pH, T, and C0 was confirmed. The effective visualization of the interaction between pairs of factors is shown in Fig. 3a-c. The contour plots of this study are shown in Fig. S8. The maximum Qe of βCCH-5 was found to be 140.9 mg/g at the optimum conditions determined by ANOVA analysis (A: pH =6; B: T = 55 °C; and C: C0 = 100 mg/L).

Temperature significantly influences the adsorption properties of βCCH-5. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Qₑ of βCCH-5 for LEV increased with a rise in temperature from 15 °C to 55 °C, confirming the endothermic nature of the adsorption process (Song et al., 2018). The thermodynamic parameters of adsorption, were calculated using Van't Hoff plot. The change in enthalpy (ΔH) was calculated to be 150.73 J/mol, which showed that the removal processes were endothermic in nature (Wang et al., 2021). Additionally, the change in entropy (ΔS) was determined to be 15.18 J/(mol K), signifying an entropy-increasing process during adsorption (Sahmoune, 2019). Furthermore, negative ΔG values at investigated temperatures indicated the spontaneous occurrence of LEV adsorption by βCCH-5 (Sahmoune, 2019). For practical applications, subsequent experiments were conducted at 25 °C.

Fig. 3e illustrates the impact of pH on the removal performance of LEV. Initially, the Qₑ of βCCH-5 increased with an initial rise in pH, reaching its peak at pH 6 (44.9 mg/g). Subsequently, Qₑ gradually decreased with further increased in pH. LEV exists in three forms: cation (LEV+), anion (LEV−), and molecule (LEV0) (Yao et al., 2021). When pH < 6.02, 6.02 < pH < 8.15, and pH > 8.15, LEV primarily existed as LEV+, LEV0, and LEV−, respectively. Simultaneously, the zero potential charge of βCCH-5 was determined to be 3.5 (Fig. S9). At pH < 3.5, βCCH-5 was electropositive, while at pH > 3.5, it was electronegative. The reduced Qₑ at pH = 2 and 10 can be attributed to electrostatic repulsion between βCCH-5 and LEV (Liu et al., 2015), while the highest Qₑ at pH = 6 was due to electrostatic attraction between βCCH-5 and LEV (Kaur et al., 2022). Consequently, a pH of 6 was selected for subsequent experiments.

Fig. 3f displays the LEV adsorption capacity of βH, βCH-5, and βCCH-5 concerning the initial concentration, with a contact time of 5 h to ensure equilibrium. Notably, βCCH-5 exhibited a higher LEV adsorption capacity than βH and βCH-5 at the same initial concentration, attributed to the loading of CNs and CDs, facilitating the adsorption process (Maged et al., 2023). The removal efficiency of βCCH-5 decreased with an increase of C0 (Fig. S10). It was found that the maximum removal efficiency reached 99.2 % at a concentration of 20 mg/L. For investigating the adsorption process, the experimental results were fitted using the Langmuir (Fig. 3h), Freundlich (Fig. 3i), Temkin (Fig. S11) and Dubinin–Radushkevich (D–R) models (Fig. S12), with fitting results shown in Table S7. Comparing the R2 values, the Langmuir model well matched the experimental data, suggesting a monolayer adsorption of LEV onto βCCH-5 (Sharma, Sharma, Laddha, Gupta, & Agarwal, 2023). Additionally, the Qm value calculated by the Langmuir isotherm model was 1376.9 mg/g, close to the actual result (1312.6 mg/g). Compared with previously developed adsorbents (Table 1 ), βCCH-5 demonstrated excellent adsorption capacity for LEV.

Fig. 3g illustrates the adsorption kinetics of LEV by βH, βCH-5, and βCCH-5 using a LEV solution with the C0 of 60 mg/L. Each hydrogel exhibited a significant initial increase followed by a gradual rise until reaching adsorption equilibrium. At any given time, βCCH-5 displayed a larger Qt than βH and βCH-5, highlighting the enhanced LEV adsorption facilitated by CNs and CDs (Dardir et al., 2021b). Meanwhile, the removal efficiency of βCCH-5 increased with an increase of t (Fig. S13). The highest removal efficiency (99.2 %) and Qt (49.83 mg/g) were achieved at 300 min when all adsorption sites of βCCH-5 were occupied. To further elucidate the adsorption characteristics of βCCH-5, the adsorption kinetics data were fitted using the Pseudo-first-order (PFO) and Pseudo-second-order (PSO) models (Guo & Wang, 2019), as depicted in Fig. 3j and k. The R2 values for the PFO and PSO models were 0.9804 and 0.9964, respectively. The fitting results (Table S8) indicated that the PSO model better matched the experimental data, indicating that the rate-limiting step may include the chemical sorption (Fan et al., 2019).

Desorption efficiency was determined using various solvents, and the optimal desorption efficiency was found in anhydrous ethanol (Fig. S14). For this, βCCH-5 underwent immersion in anhydrous ethanol for 5 h, followed by five washes with ultrapure water. Subsequently, the next adsorption experiments were conducted using the regenerated βCCH-5. Fig. 4a illustrates five consecutive adsorption-desorption cycles of βCCH-5. After five cycles, βCCH-5 maintained approximately 90.4 % of its initial adsorption capacity, with a removal efficiency still maintaining almost 97.3 %. This stability was likely attributed to the 3D porous structure of βCCH-5 (Zhang et al., 2022). Furthermore, XRD and FTIR analyses of βCCH-5 after five recycling studies were performed (Fig. 4b and c). The XRD spectra of recycled βCCH-5 showed no significant changes compared to that of βCCH-5, indicating the preservation of crystallinity and structural integrity. The chemical structure of βCCH-5 remained similar to the original after five adsorption-desorption cycles, as confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy (Luo et al., 2022). This observation provides conclusive evidence of the excellent stability of the prepared βCCH-5.

The adsorption of LEV in real water was carried out to evaluate the efficacy of βCCH-5 in practical application. The key quality parameters of these real water samples are summarized in Table S9. In this context, 30 mg of βCCH-5 was introduced into a 100 mL solution containing 60 mg/L of LEV for a contact time of 5 h. The variation in the adsorbed amount of LEV between the synthetic solution (DI water containing LEV) and the two real water samples was observed. For LEV (Qₑ = 178.6 mg/g in the synthetic solution), βCCH-5 exhibited a reduction in efficacy by 20.1 % (Qₑ = 142.7 mg/g) in tap water and 23.2 % (Qₑ = 137.2 mg/g) in lake water. Despite experiencing a certain percentage loss in efficacy from tap water to lake water, βCCH-5 still demonstrates a significant adsorption capacity across various types of water samples, supporting its potential for practical application (Deng et al., 2023).

To thoroughly assess the selectivity in detecting LEV, we investigated the fluorescence variations upon introducing various antibiotics, including LEV, Tetracycline (TC), sulfadiazine (SD), oxytetracycline (OTC), minocycline (MINO), doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline (CTC), chloramphenicol (CAP), ciprofloxacin (CIP), and norfloxacin (NOR) into βCCH-5. The molecular structures of these antibiotics were shown in Fig. S15. The fluorescence variations of βCCH-5 were recorded by the camera, as illustrated in Fig. 5a. Notably, only the presence of LEV significantly enhanced the fluorescence, while the other antibiotics had minimal or no impact on the fluorescence of βCCH-5. These fluorescence variations were quantified using AQY/AQY₀, where AQY represents the absolute quantum yield of βCCH-5 with antibiotics, as depicted in Fig. 5b. The results indicated the high selectivity of βCCH-5 towards LEV (Tan & Zhou, 2024). Similar selectivity was observed in the prepared CDs (Fig. S16).

Anti-interference capability of the βCCH-5/LEV system was further investigated, and the presence of coexisting antibiotics (TC, SD, OTC, MINO, DOX, CTC, CAP, CIP, and NOR) resulted in minimal changes in the fluorescent signal (Fig. S17). Among the tested antibiotics, LEV exhibited the highest response, indicating βCCH-5 exhibited effective anti-interference capabilities.

The detection conditions were optimized firstly (Wang, Lian, & Yan, 2019)(Wang, Zhu, et al., 2019). As depicted in Fig. S18, the AQY of βCCH-5 remained consistent in the temperature range of 15–55 °C. For experimental convenience, 25 °C was chosen as the optimal temperature. In Fig. S19, the AQY of βCCH-5 gradually decreased and then stabilized within the reaction time range of 1–5 min. Based on practical considerations, the reaction time was set at 2 min (Liu et al., 2018). Additionally, the fluorescence response of βCCH-5 to LEV varied with the pH range from 2 to 10, reaching a peak at pH 6 (Fig. S20). In summary, the detection system operated at pH 6, 25 °C, for 3 min.

For the accurate determination of LEV, the concentration of LEV was quantitatively added to βCCH-5 under optimal conditions. Fig. 6a illustrated the color changes of βCCH-5 with varying concentrations of LEV. Additionally, a strong linear relationship was observed between the AQY and various LEV concentrations in the range from 1 to 120 mg/L. The resulting linear regression equation was AQY = −0.0193 × C₀ + 6.0578, with an associated R2 of 0.9981. The LOD, calculated based on 3σ/k (Gao et al., 2022), was determined to be 0.76 mg/L. As depicted in Fig. 6b, with a gradual increase in LEV concentration, the fluorescence intensity of βCCH-5 also increased. Fig. 6b demonstrated a linear correlation between F0/F-1 and the concentration within the range of 0.2–1.0 μg/L, expressed by the linear equation F0/F-1 = 0.0868 × C₀ + 0.0017 (R2 = 0.9955). Here, the LOD was calculated to be 0.09 μg/L, which was lower than that of the prepared CDs (Fig. S21). Furthermore, βCCH-5 exhibited a broad linear range and enhanced sensitivity when compared to the majority of fluorescence-based approaches for detecting LEV as shown in Table 2 .

The fluorescence detection of LEV by βCCH-5 in real water samples was established, and its reliability was assessed (see Table S10). The recoveries of the spiked samples ranged from 96.47 % to 106.42 %, with an RSD of ≤4.24 %. These results affirm the practicality and reliability of βCCH-5 for LEV detection in real water samples (Bhogal et al., 2024).

To further investigate the adsorption mechanism of βCCH-5 with LEV, FTIR and XPS analyses were conducted before and after adsorption. As illustrated in Fig. 7a, after LEV adsorption, the intensity of the O − H stretching vibration peak at 3206 cm−1 decreased, indicating the formation of hydrogen bonding between OH (βCCH-5) and fluorine/nitrogen (from LEV) (Maged et al., 2023). Conversely, the increased intensity of the N − H stretching vibration peak at 1523 cm−1 highlighted the crucial role of amino groups in the adsorption of LEV by βCCH-5 (Chi et al., 2022).

In Fig. 7b, the elemental composition analysis of βCCH-5 indicated the presence of oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and carbon (C). The N content showed an increase post-adsorption, indicating the successful adsorption of LEV onto βCCH-5 (Yang, Li, et al., 2020). The O1s spectrum in Fig. 7c displayed two distinct peaks at 531.9 and 533.2 corresponding to CO and CO, respectively (Li et al., 2021)(Li et al., 2021). After adsorption, the CO peak decreased, possibly due to π-π interactions between LEV and βCCH-5 (Yao et al., 2021). The N1s spectrum in Fig. 7d, ranging from 395 to 405 eV, revealed three peaks: –N = at 398.8 eV, –NH2 at 399.5 eV, and –NH3 + at 400.9 eV. Post-adsorption, –NH2 shifted to 399.7 eV, indicating hydrogen bonding between βCCH-5 and LEV. Moreover, –NH3 + shifted from 400.9 to 401.2 eV, suggesting electrostatic interactions between –NH3 + and LEV (Luo et al., 2022). Fig. 7e displayed the C1s spectrum with characteristic peaks assigned to CC (284.9 eV), CO (286.8 eV), and O–C=O (289.1 eV) (Wan et al., 2022). Simultaneously, these peaks shifted to lower binding energy, possibly due to the π-π interactions (Wang & Jang, 2022). In summary, the results confirmed that LEV interacted with βCCH-5 through electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding (Maged et al., 2023), and π-π interactions (Yao et al., 2021).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of βCCH-5 by LEV can be investigated using UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 8a, the UV–vis absorption band of LEV overlapped with the fluorescence excitation band of βCCH-5, indicating a reduction in the excitation of βCCH-5. This observation suggested a potential mechanism stemming from internal filtration effect (IFE) (Shi et al., 2022). Additionally, UV–vis absorption spectra of βCCH-5 in the presence and absence of LEV were recorded (Fig. S22). The results showed that the absorption peak decreased significantly. Fig. 8b illustrates the change in the fluorescence lifetime of βCCH-5 before and after the introduction of LEV (Liu, Li, et al., 2022). There was almost no change in fluorescence lifetime, going from 4.14 to 4.08 ns following the reduce of LEV (Fig. 8c and d), further confirming static quenching (Liu, Ye-Tao, et al., 2022). In summary, the fluorescence quenching of βCCH-5 was attributed to IFE and static quenching.

Fig. 9 provides a concise explanation of the mechanism underlying LEV adsorption and detection by βCCH. The amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of βCCH facilitate LEV enrichment through chemical interactions (Luo et al., 2022). Subsequently, LEV enters the 3D porous structure of βCCH. During the diffusion process, some LEV is adsorbed onto internal adsorption sites through electrostatic interaction (Luo et al., 2022), H-bonding (Maged et al., 2023), and π-π interaction (Yao et al., 2021) with the mechanism of monolayer adsorption (Sharma, Sharma, Laddha, Agarwal, & Gupta, 2023). The introduction of CNs establishes the skeleton of the hydrogel, while the loading of CDs introduces additional sorption sites, collectively enhancing βCCH's adsorption capacity. Moreover, CDs serve as a fluorescence sensor for LEV detection (Sun & Lei, 2017). The incorporation of CDs into the 3D porous structure of βCCH further bolsters the stability of CDs (Cheng et al., 2019).

The cost analysis of βCCH-5 was conducted to assess its practical feasibility (Murray et al., 2023). Utilizing established cost-estimation procedures (see Table S11), βCCH-5 cost CNY 27.52 a kilogram, accounting for only 18.66 % of commercial activated carbon (CNY 147.50). Considering the Qm value of 1376.9 mg/g for βCCH-5, the cost for removing LEV using βCCH-5 was calculated at CNY 0.02/g (refer to Table S12). In contrast, the cost of commercialized activated carbon was significantly higher at CNY 1.47/g. This analysis supports the conclusion that βCCH-5 presents itself as a promising and cost-effective adsorbent for LEV removal from wastewater, highlighting both environmental and financial benefits.

An innovative and efficient βCCH was synthesized for the simultaneous detection and adsorption of LEV. Adsorption experiments demonstrated that LEV adsorption by βCCH followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and the Langmuir model. The results of the ANOVA analysis showed that the pH, temperature, and initial concentration of LEV were statistically significant in the adsorption of LEV by βCCH. βCCH exhibited a maximum adsorption capacity of 1376.9 mg/g, attributed to the synergistic effects of CNs and CDs. Batch adsorption studies demonstrated that βCCH achieved a removal efficiency of 99.2 % under the following conditions: pH = 6, initial concentration = 20 mg/L, contact time = 300 min, and temperature = 25 °C. XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed the adsorption mechanism of βCCH, involving electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interactions. Furthermore, βCCH served as a fluorescence sensor for detecting LEV with a low detection limit of 0.09 μg/L. In real-water environments, βCCH demonstrated a recovery rate ranging from 96.47 % to 106.42 %. Economic analysis affirmed that the cost of βCCH for LEV removal was significantly lower than that of commercialized activated carbon. Consequently, βCCH is positioned as a cost-effective and reusable adsorbent for the simultaneous detection and removal of antibiotics.
